quotes.topic: Minor II V I blues scale usage
quotes.verbatim_quote:
On a minor II V I, this pattern can also be played starting on the b7 of the dominant chord (bar 2)
or the 4 of the minor chord (bar 4).

Used like this, the pattern comes out of the A minor blues scale.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the substitution logic for placing a blues-scale pattern across minor II V I chord changes, giving players two specific entry points.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Pattern 27 is based around the upper notes of a C triad.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the structural basis of Pattern 27 as a triad voicing concept, anchoring how to visualize and apply it.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Pattern 28 is another dominant phrase based around a Bm7b5 arpeggio.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the upper-structure arpeggio substitution rule (Bm7b5 over G7) that is a core jazz harmony device.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The technique of pattern 29 is often used in gypsy jazz.
Each note of the F triad used in this pattern is approached from a chromatic note below.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names and defines the chromatic-approach technique and identifies its stylistic home, giving players both the method and the context.

quotes.topic: Major triad sub for minor chords
quotes.verbatim_quote:
Maj7 chords (or major triads) are a common substitution for minor chords.

You can play a major triad or maj7 chord on the third of a major chord.
For example: Fmaj7 (or F) over Dm7
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the rule for major-triad-over-minor substitution and gives the concrete interval (third) from which to build it.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This 3157 motif is a good way to start a phrase because it ends on the leading tone of the key.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives a functional reason (leading-tone resolution) for using this specific note-order motif as a phrase opener.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Pattern 32 has a typical bebop rhythm with string skipping.

This pattern works for major (Cmaj7) and dominant (C7) chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines both the rhythmic character and the chord-type flexibility of Pattern 32, two essential application rules.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can play an m(add9) over altered dominant chords, starting on the b9.

For example: Fm(add9) over E7#9
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the exact substitution rule (m(add9) arpeggio from b9 of the altered dominant) that makes Pattern 33 work across chord types.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can play a m(add9) arpeggio over half-diminished chords, starting on the b3.
For example: Dm(add9) over Bm7b5.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Extends the m(add9) substitution rule to half-diminished chords with a specific interval entry point (b3), completing the II V I application.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Augmented chords are often used to play over minor chords, resulting in a minor/major 7 sound.

Over a minor chord you can play an augmented triad or maj7#5 arpeggio, both built on the b3

of the minor chord.
For example: Gaug or Gmaj7#5 over Em7
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the rule and sonic result of the augmented-over-minor substitution, including which scale degree to build from.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The convenience about augmented triads is that its inversions look exactly the same, so you can just
slide the same shape 4 frets up or down and essentially get the same chord:
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives a practical fingering rule unique to augmented symmetry that simplifies fretboard navigation.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Augmented triads can also be used to solo over dominant #11 chords, which are usually the result
of a tritone substitution. For example: Db7#11 going to Cmaj7

Because Db7#11 is a substitution for G7alt, you can use a G augmented triad over that chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Connects tritone substitution theory to augmented triad usage, giving players a concrete harmonic reason and target chord for the application.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The 1235 pattern (aka the Coltrane pattern) is a four-note motif that consists of the 1st, 2nd, 3rd,
and 5th notes of a scale.

These patterns were popularized by John Coltrane, who used them on his Giant Steps solo.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names, defines, and attributes the 1235 motif, establishing its identity and historical context as a core jazz vocabulary element.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Because the 1235 pattern doesn’t have a 7 or b7, it can be used over major as well as dominant
chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the theoretical reason why the 1235 pattern is harmonically flexible, teaching players to reason about interval content rather than memorize chord types.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Dominants going to major chords can use the regular 1235 pattern.

Dominants going to minor chords require a b9 instead of a 9.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives a clear decision rule for choosing between 1235 and 1 b2 3 5 based on the target chord quality (major vs. minor resolution).
